dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for unknown problem/person down.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. Let's assess the situation.
Uh, a man was found unresponsive on the floor, lying in a pool of blood right here at the counter inside.
Got it. Has anyone seen what caused this?
No, not really. We just saw him on the floor like that. We didn't see any signs of struggle or anything.
Is the patient responding to anything now?
No, not at first. He was just lying there. Then, when my coworker touched him, he tried to move.
Let me try to assess him. Patient, can you tell me your name? Are you okay?
He's attempting to move his arms. Let's perform a jaw thrust to check airway and c-spine.
Let's maintain c-spine. Jaw thrust maneuver, anyone?
Jaw thrust is complete. Airway is open, but he's snoring. Let's secure the x-collar and consider BVM oxygen.
Snoring respirations. Due to head injury, NPA is contraindicated. Let's proceed with BVM at 25 LPM.
Let's apply the x-collar now.
X-collar applied and secured.
Snoring respirations. Initiate BVM oxygen now.
Initial respirations are 20 per minute. Holding BVM via mask.
Radial pulse is present and rapid.
Unable to obtain BP at initial contact.
SpO2 is 0%. Administering oxygen via BVM should resolve this.
Respirations remain at 20 per minute.
Radial pulse now measurable at 124.
Blood pressure has risen to 189/138 mmHg.
SpO2 has increased to 99% with oxygen administration.
Blood is present at the scene, mostly in large clots. Blood loss estimated at 50 to 100mL. Check for signs of external injury.
Head laceration on right parietal area, 2-3 cm in size with no active bleeding observed.
C-spine manual stabilization performed. Continue monitoring and securing.
Medic record shows history of epilepsy, hypertension, and hyperlipidemia.
Current meds include Keppra, Coreg, Vytorin, and Prinivil. No allergies noted.
We didn't see how this happened. He was fine until we found him here.
And the patient didn't remember anything?
According to the report, he only remembered waking up to us on scene.
Respiratory rate has dropped to 12 per minute.
Let's place an IV now to secure vascular access.
IV has been placed, 18G, required two attempts.
ePCR notes a pain level of 10 out of 10 reported.
Pulse is now 120 at last reading.
Respiratory rate increased to 33.
Blood pressure is 186/130 currently.
SpO2 is now 94%. Continue to monitor closely.
Apply 4-lead ECG monitoring as noted.
ECG reading still shows sinus tachycardia. 4 lead monitoring applied per protocol.
Proceeding with head injury protocol-spine restriction and BP monitoring.
Applying cervical spinal restriction of motion.
Soft restraints applied to all limbs due to patient's combative and flailing behavior.
Patient is yelling, "I can't breathe," and attempting to remove the BVM while confused.
Patient shows altered mental status (AMS) per ePCR.
Glucose measurement is not available and remains at 0.
Current vitals: Pulse 117, respirations 26, BP 173/78, SpO2 100%. EKG shows sinus tachycardia.
Patient is stable for transport. No changes in ABCs.
Transporting to [COMPANY_NAME] for head injury.
Yes, it's best for serious head injuries. Using a reeves sleeve per protocol to ensure stability due to flailing.
All restraints in place. Final check of vitals and gear.
All vitals remaining stable. Transport readiness confirmed.
Vitals remain stable. BP 173/78, SpO2 100%, oxygen still on and IV secure.
Rapid extrication using a reeves sleeve was performed to avoid risk of c-spine injury and to allow stability due to patient flailing.
Backboard not used due to patient's agitation and location at the scene necessitating rapid extrication using a reeves sleeve.
Skin temperature could not be determined due to the presence of clots.
Rechecking the patient's breathing rate now with current vitals in mind.
Respiratory rate has slowed to 12 breaths per minute. Per ePCR record.
Patient was agitated. Full breath sounds couldn't be obtained. Logically, no clear breath sound abnormalities recorded.
Unable to perform a detailed exam due to combative behavior. Basic observations unremarkable.
Rechecking BP after movement. Reading is 173/78, which is stable.
BVM reapplied due to patient's discomfort with the facepiece.
Airway still open with occasional snoring. BVM at 25 LPM oxygen.
Pain at 10/10 reported. Perception remains unchanged.
Patient unable to provide consistent history. Referenced bystander statements and prior report.
BP and other vitals stable during ambulance loading.
Full monitoring in place. Patient's mental status improved slightly en route.
Crew continues monitoring and BVM is reapplied as necessary. Ready to roll.
Patient is ready for transport. All lines are secured and functioning.
Continued assisted breathing. Respiratory rate remains at 26.
SpO2 is 100% with 25 LPM oxygen via mask.
No further changes during transport. Patient stable with BVM off for most of the ride.
